title: Organelles and compartmentalization
summary: B2.2 is where cell structure becomes cell logic. This lesson works through the structure and function of every major organelle you are expected to know — nucleus and nucleolus, rough and smooth endoplasmic reticulum, ribosomes, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, chloroplasts, lysosomes, vesicles, vacuole, cell wall, plasma membrane and cytoskeleton — and then shows why eukaryotic cells gain from packaging their chemistry into membrane-bound compartments. It follows a secreted protein along the endomembrane pathway (ribosome to rough ER to Golgi to vesicle to plasma membrane) and lays out the compartmentalization argument the way our Paper 2 marking engine actually awards the marks: one credit per distinct valid advantage, with error-carried-forward.
learningObjectives: State the structure and function of the major eukaryotic organelles and cell structures: nucleus, nucleolus, rough and smooth ER, ribosomes, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, chloroplasts, lysosomes, vesicles, vacuole, cell wall, plasma membrane and cytoskeleton.

Explain the advantages of compartmentalization: separating incompatible reactions, concentrating enzymes and substrates, isolating harmful substances, increasing membrane surface area and localising conditions such as pH.
Describe the endomembrane system and trace the pathway of a secreted protein from ribosome to plasma membrane.
Structure an explain-type answer so that each distinct valid point earns a mark under analytic marking with error-carried-forward.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: A Cell Built From Rooms
simpleExplanation.summary: A eukaryotic cell is not one open space full of chemicals sloshing together. Internal membranes divide it into compartments called organelles, each a room built for one kind of job with its own contents, enzymes and conditions. That division of labour is what makes a large, complex cell run efficiently.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a hospital rather than a single first-aid tent. In an open tent, surgery, laundry, cooking and the pharmacy would all share the same air and the same bench — sterile work would be contaminated, and chemicals meant for one task would react with the wrong things. A hospital solves this with separate rooms: an operating theatre kept sterile, a pharmacy where drugs are concentrated and shelved, an incinerator that safely destroys waste behind a sealed door. Each room holds its own equipment and its own conditions. The eukaryotic cell does exactly this with membranes: the nucleus guards the DNA, mitochondria run respiration, lysosomes do the dangerous digestion behind an acidic wall, and the rough ER and Golgi form a production line for proteins.
steps: Membranes divide the cytoplasm into separate compartments, so several different processes can run at the same time without interfering.
Each compartment concentrates the specific enzymes and substrates for one pathway, which raises the reaction rate.
Each compartment can hold its own conditions — for example the acidic interior of a lysosome — tuned to what happens inside it.
Dangerous reactions and destructive enzymes are sealed away from the rest of the cell, protecting the cytoplasm and its contents.

flashcards.back: Double membrane (nuclear envelope) pierced by nuclear pores; contains the chromosomes (DNA + histone proteins). Stores the genetic information and controls the cell by regulating which genes are transcribed. mRNA leaves through the pores.
A dense region inside the nucleus, not membrane-bound. Site where ribosomal RNA is transcribed and ribosome subunits are assembled. A prominent nucleolus indicates a cell making many ribosomes.

Site of protein synthesis (translation). Made of rRNA and protein, with a large and small subunit; NOT membrane-bound, so not counted as a compartment. Found free in the cytoplasm and bound to the rough ER. Present in prokaryotes too.
A network of membrane sacs studded with ribosomes. Synthesises and folds proteins destined for secretion, for the plasma membrane or for other organelles, and passes them on in transport vesicles.

Membrane network WITHOUT ribosomes. Synthesises lipids and steroids, metabolises some drugs and toxins, and (in muscle) stores calcium ions. Do not confuse its job with the protein role of the rough ER.

A stack of flattened membrane sacs (cisternae). Receives proteins from the rER, then modifies, sorts and packages them into vesicles for secretion or delivery elsewhere. The cell's processing and dispatch department.

Double membrane; the inner membrane is folded into cristae to increase surface area for the electron transport chain, enclosing the fluid matrix. Site of aerobic respiration and most ATP production. Abundant in energy-demanding cells.

Found in plant and algal cells. Double membrane; internal stacks of thylakoid membranes (grana) hold chlorophyll for the light-dependent reactions, surrounded by the fluid stroma where the light-independent reactions fix carbon. Site of photosynthesis.

Single-membrane sac of hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes held at an acidic pH (~4.5–5). Breaks down worn-out organelles, ingested material and waste. Its membrane keeps these destructive enzymes away from the cytoplasm.

A small membrane-bound sac that transports materials between organelles and to or from the plasma membrane. The 'delivery vans' of the endomembrane system; secretory vesicles fuse with the membrane to release contents by exocytosis.

A fluid-filled membrane-bound sac. In plant cells a large central vacuole stores water and solutes and generates turgor pressure that supports the cell; in animal cells vacuoles are small and used for storage or transport.

Rigid layer OUTSIDE the plasma membrane (cellulose in plants, chitin in fungi, peptidoglycan in bacteria). Provides support and shape and resists bursting when the cell takes in water. Not present in animal cells.

The phospholipid bilayer that surrounds every cell and controls what enters and leaves. Selectively permeable, holding the cell contents together and separating the internal environment from the outside.

A network of protein filaments (microtubules and microfilaments) through the cytoplasm. Maintains cell shape, anchors and moves organelles, provides tracks for vesicle transport and drives cell movement and division.

Dividing the cell interior into separate membrane-bound compartments. Advantages: separates incompatible reactions, concentrates enzymes and substrates, isolates harmful substances, adds membrane surface area, and lets each compartment hold its own optimal conditions.

The set of membranes that work together to make, modify and move proteins and lipids: nuclear envelope, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, vesicles and the plasma membrane. Lysosomes are formed from it.

flashcards.front: Common Trap: is a ribosome an organelle?
flashcards.back: For compartmentalization, organelles are MEMBRANE-BOUND compartments. Ribosomes have no membrane, so they are not compartments — and they cannot provide the isolation that compartmentalization is about. They are essential structures, but do not cite them as examples of compartmentalization.

No. Prokaryotes have no membrane-bound organelles and no nucleus — their DNA lies free in the cytoplasm and their reactions share one compartment. Only eukaryotes gain the advantages of compartmentalization. Saying bacteria have mitochondria or a nucleus loses marks.

quickCheck.prompt: Which organelle modifies, sorts and packages proteins into vesicles after they arrive from the rough ER?
options: Smooth endoplasmic reticulum
Golgi apparatus
Lysosome
Nucleolus

quickCheck.prompt: Place the organelles a secreted protein passes through in the correct order.
options: Golgi apparatus → ribosome → rough ER → vesicle → plasma membrane
Ribosome → rough ER → Golgi apparatus → vesicle → plasma membrane
Rough ER → ribosome → vesicle → Golgi apparatus → plasma membrane
Ribosome → Golgi apparatus → rough ER → lysosome → plasma membrane
quickCheck.answer: Ribosome → rough ER → Golgi apparatus → vesicle → plasma membrane

quickCheck.prompt: Why does concentrating a pathway's enzymes and substrates inside one compartment increase the reaction rate?
options: It raises the temperature inside the organelle
It brings enzymes and substrates closer together, so they collide and react more frequently
It removes the need for enzymes altogether
It makes the membrane thicker and stronger
quickCheck.answer: It brings enzymes and substrates closer together, so they collide and react more frequently

quickCheck.prompt: A lysosome keeps its interior at about pH 4.5. This is an example of which advantage of compartmentalization?
options: Increasing membrane surface area for reactions
Localising optimal conditions for the enzymes inside
Storing genetic information safely
Reflecting incoming radiation
quickCheck.answer: Localising optimal conditions for the enzymes inside

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement about prokaryotic cells is correct?
options: They compartmentalise metabolism using many small mitochondria
They lack membrane-bound organelles, so they cannot compartmentalise metabolism this way
They use a nuclear envelope to protect their DNA
Their Golgi apparatus packages secreted proteins
quickCheck.answer: They lack membrane-bound organelles, so they cannot compartmentalise metabolism this way

faq.q: What exactly counts as 'compartmentalization' in the mark scheme?
faq.a: It is the division of the cell into separate compartments by internal membranes. The examinable advantages are a fixed list: separating incompatible reactions, concentrating enzymes and substrates to raise reaction rate, isolating harmful substances or enzymes, increasing the membrane surface area available for membrane-bound reactions, and allowing each compartment to hold its own conditions such as pH. When a question asks you to explain the advantages, it is essentially asking you to name and justify items from this list — each distinct one is worth a mark.

faq.q: Are ribosomes, the cell wall and the cytoskeleton organelles?
faq.a: They are examinable cell structures you must know, but they are not membrane-bound compartments, so they do not illustrate compartmentalization. Ribosomes are the site of translation, the cell wall is an external support layer, and the cytoskeleton is an internal protein scaffold. Keep them separate in your mind from the membrane-bound organelles (nucleus, ER, Golgi, mitochondria, chloroplasts, lysosomes, vacuole) when a question is specifically about compartmentalization.

faq.q: How is the rough ER different from the smooth ER?
faq.a: Both are networks of membrane sacs, but the rough ER is coated with ribosomes and handles proteins — synthesising, folding and forwarding proteins bound for secretion or membranes. The smooth ER has no ribosomes and handles lipids: it makes phospholipids and steroids, processes some drugs and toxins, and in muscle stores calcium. A cell that secretes a lot of protein has abundant rough ER; a cell that makes a lot of lipid or steroid hormone has abundant smooth ER.

faq.q: Why can't a prokaryote just do everything a eukaryote does without organelles?
faq.a: Prokaryotes carry out respiration and many reactions successfully in a single shared compartment because they are small, with a high surface-area-to-volume ratio and a smaller range of simultaneous pathways. A large eukaryotic cell running many incompatible reactions at once would suffer interference and slow reaction rates without internal separation. Compartmentalization is the structural solution that lets eukaryotic cells grow larger and more metabolically complex than prokaryotes.

sections.content: The single biggest structural difference between a prokaryotic cell and a eukaryotic cell is not size or shape — it is internal organisation. A prokaryote runs its chemistry in one shared space. A eukaryote divides its interior with membranes into compartments called organelles, each a specialised workshop with its own contents, enzymes and conditions. This lesson does two things. First it sets out the structure and function of every organelle and cell structure you are expected to know, always linking structure to what the structure is for. Then it explains WHY compartmentalization is such an advantage, follows a secreted protein along the endomembrane pathway, and shows exactly how our marking engine awards the marks on the classic 'advantages of compartmentalization' question — because in an explain-type topic, how you structure the answer decides how many marks you keep.

Compartmentalization is the division of a eukaryotic cell's interior into separate membrane-bound compartments. Each compartment — each organelle — is enclosed by at least one membrane that controls what passes in and out, so it can hold its own set of enzymes, its own substrates and its own conditions independent of the rest of the cell. This is the structural basis for a cell running dozens of different chemical processes at the same time without them interfering. It is worth being precise from the start: the compartments that matter here are membrane-bound. Ribosomes, the cytoskeleton and the cell wall are essential structures but are not membrane-bound compartments, so they are not what a compartmentalization question is asking about.
The major organelles and cell structures

Every organelle is shaped for its job, and exam marks come from linking a named structural feature to the function it serves. Read each of the following as a structure-to-function pair rather than a label to memorise.

items: **Nucleus:** enclosed by a double membrane (nuclear envelope) pierced by pores; holds the chromosomes and controls the cell by regulating gene transcription. mRNA exits through the pores to the cytoplasm.
**Nucleolus:** a dense, non-membrane-bound region inside the nucleus where ribosomal RNA is made and ribosome subunits are assembled.
**Ribosomes:** the site of protein synthesis (translation); made of rRNA and protein, with no membrane. Found free in the cytoplasm and attached to the rough ER. Present in prokaryotes as well.

**Rough endoplasmic reticulum (rER):** membrane network studded with ribosomes; synthesises and folds proteins destined for secretion, the plasma membrane or other organelles, then buds them off in vesicles.
**Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (sER):** membrane network without ribosomes; synthesises lipids and steroids, processes some toxins and drugs, and stores calcium in muscle.
**Golgi apparatus:** a stack of flattened cisternae that modifies, sorts and packages proteins from the rER into vesicles for secretion or delivery.

**Mitochondrion:** double membrane with the inner membrane folded into cristae to enlarge surface area for the electron transport chain, enclosing the fluid matrix; site of aerobic respiration and most ATP production.

**Chloroplast (plant and algal cells):** double membrane with internal thylakoid stacks (grana) holding chlorophyll for the light-dependent reactions, surrounded by the stroma where carbon is fixed; site of photosynthesis.
**Lysosome:** single-membrane sac of hydrolytic enzymes held at an acidic pH; digests worn-out organelles, ingested material and waste while keeping those enzymes away from the cytoplasm.
**Vesicles:** small membrane sacs that move materials between organelles and to the plasma membrane; secretory vesicles fuse with the membrane to release contents by exocytosis.
**Vacuole:** a fluid-filled membrane sac; the large central vacuole of a plant cell stores solutes and creates turgor pressure that supports the cell.
**Cell wall:** a rigid layer outside the plasma membrane (cellulose in plants) giving support and shape and preventing the cell bursting; absent in animal cells.
**Plasma membrane:** the selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer surrounding the cell, controlling exchange with the environment.
**Cytoskeleton:** a network of protein filaments that maintains shape, anchors and moves organelles, and provides tracks along which vesicles are transported.

sections.content: When a question asks you to describe an organelle, always pair a structural feature with its function — 'cristae increase surface area for the electron transport chain', not just 'has cristae'. Use the precise terms: cristae for the inner mitochondrial folds, cisternae for the flattened ER and Golgi sacs, stroma for chloroplast fluid, matrix for mitochondrial fluid. Vague words like 'folds' or 'liquid' often miss the mark.

Sealing reactions into separate membrane-bound compartments gives a eukaryotic cell several concrete advantages, and these are the exact points an exam answer must draw on. First, incompatible reactions can run at the same time without interfering: a lysosome can hydrolyse macromolecules while, a short distance away, the cytoplasm builds those same kinds of molecules up. Second, enzymes and substrates for one pathway are concentrated in a small volume, so they collide and react more often — the reaction rate rises. Third, harmful substances and destructive enzymes are isolated: the acid hydrolases of a lysosome are kept behind a membrane so they cannot digest the rest of the cell. Fourth, folded internal membranes provide a large surface area for the membrane-bound steps of pathways, such as the electron transport chain on the mitochondrial cristae. Fifth, each compartment can hold its own local conditions — the low pH inside a lysosome, for instance — set to the optimum for the reactions it contains.

items: **Separating incompatible reactions:** processes that would interfere, such as synthesis and digestion, run simultaneously in different compartments.
**Concentrating enzymes and substrates:** confining a pathway to a small volume increases collision frequency and raises the reaction rate.
**Isolating harmful substances:** destructive enzymes and toxins (e.g. lysosomal hydrolases) are sealed away, protecting the rest of the cell.
**Increasing membrane surface area:** internal membranes (e.g. mitochondrial cristae, thylakoids) provide more area for membrane-bound reactions.
**Localising optimal conditions:** each compartment can maintain its own pH or ion concentration suited to its reactions (e.g. acidic lysosomes).

sections.content: The endomembrane system and the secretion pathway

Several organelles are not independent workshops but stages on one production line — the endomembrane system, made up of the nuclear envelope, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, vesicles and the plasma membrane. Its most examined job is the manufacture and export of a secreted protein such as the hormone insulin or a digestive enzyme. The route is fixed and worth learning as a sequence: a ribosome on the rough ER translates the protein; it enters the rough ER, where it is folded and modified; a transport vesicle buds off and carries it to the Golgi apparatus; the Golgi modifies, sorts and packages it; a secretory vesicle carries it to the plasma membrane; the vesicle fuses with the membrane and releases the protein outside the cell by exocytosis. Notice how compartmentalization underlies the whole route — each stage happens in its own compartment, and the vesicles move cargo between them without it ever mixing freely with the cytoplasm.

sections.question: A pancreatic cell secretes the protein hormone insulin. Describe the pathway taken by an insulin molecule from its synthesis to its release from the cell, naming the organelles involved and the role of each. [5]
sections.solution:
Trace the molecule stage by stage, naming the compartment AND what it does there — each named organelle with its correct role is a marking point.

**Stage 1 — Ribosome (on the rough ER).** The insulin polypeptide is synthesised by translation on a ribosome attached to the rough ER. [1: ribosome = site of protein synthesis]

**Stage 2 — Rough endoplasmic reticulum.** The new polypeptide enters the rough ER, where it is folded and modified into its correct shape, then packaged into a transport vesicle that buds off. [1: rER folds/modifies protein and forms transport vesicle]

**Stage 3 — Golgi apparatus.** The transport vesicle carries the protein to the Golgi apparatus, which further modifies, processes and packages it, sorting it into a secretory vesicle. [1: Golgi modifies and packages]

**Stage 4 — Secretory vesicle.** A secretory vesicle carries the finished insulin to the plasma membrane. [1: vesicle transports to membrane]

**Stage 5 — Plasma membrane.** The vesicle fuses with the plasma membrane and releases the insulin outside the cell by exocytosis. [1: exocytosis at the plasma membrane]

**Answer:** ribosome → rough ER → Golgi apparatus → secretory vesicle → plasma membrane (exocytosis).

*The order is fixed and the direction matters. A common lost mark is putting the Golgi before the rough ER, or omitting the vesicle steps that link the compartments — the vesicles are what make this a compartmentalised pathway rather than one open reaction.*

sections.question: An electron micrograph shows a mitochondrion measured at 4.5 cm long. The scale bar beside it represents 1 µm and measures 3.0 cm. Calculate the actual length of the mitochondrion in micrometres. [3]
sections.solution:
Work out the magnification from the scale bar first, then use it on the mitochondrion.

**Step 1 — magnification from the scale bar.** Magnification = image size ÷ actual size. Convert to the same unit: 3.0 cm = 30 000 µm, and the bar represents 1 µm.
$M = \dfrac{30\,000\ \text{µm}}{1\ \text{µm}} = 30\,000\times$. [1: correct magnification]

**Step 2 — actual length of the mitochondrion.** Actual size = image size ÷ magnification. Image length 4.5 cm = 45 000 µm.
$A = \dfrac{45\,000\ \text{µm}}{30\,000} = 1.5\ \text{µm}$. [1: correct substitution]

**Answer:** the mitochondrion is 1.5 µm long. [1: correct value WITH the unit]

*Always carry the unit through to the final line — an answer of '1.5' with no unit is not a complete answer, and mismatched units (cm against µm) is the usual reason this calculation goes wrong.*

items: **Citing ribosomes, the cell wall or the cytoskeleton as examples of compartmentalization** — they are not membrane-bound compartments, so they cannot provide the isolation the concept is about. Use membrane-bound organelles for these answers.

**Claiming prokaryotes compartmentalise their metabolism** — they have no membrane-bound organelles and no nucleus; their DNA and reactions share one compartment. Only eukaryotes gain these advantages.
**Reversing the secretion pathway** — the fixed order is ribosome → rough ER → Golgi → vesicle → plasma membrane. Putting the Golgi before the rough ER, or dropping the vesicle steps, loses marks.
**Confusing rough and smooth ER** — rough ER (with ribosomes) handles PROTEINS; smooth ER (no ribosomes) handles LIPIDS. Swapping their roles is a frequent error.
**Saying compartmentalization raises the rate 'by raising temperature'** — it raises the rate by concentrating enzymes and substrates so collisions are more frequent, not by heating anything.
**Describing organelles with a bare label** — 'has cristae' or 'has grana' scores little; you must link the feature to its function ('cristae increase surface area for the electron transport chain').
**Mixing up the fluids and folds** — matrix (mitochondrion) vs stroma (chloroplast); cristae (mitochondrion) vs thylakoids/grana (chloroplast). Precise terms are required for full marks.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

The advantages-of-compartmentalization question is an explain-type question, and the marks are awarded analytically: each distinct valid advantage you give, correctly linked to why it helps the cell, scores one mark up to the total available. Method marks (M) credit a correct advantage with its reasoning; answer marks (A) credit a correct statement of an advantage; error-carried-forward (ECF) means one weak point does not sink the ones that follow. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific named advantage rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain the advantages to a eukaryotic cell of compartmentalising its metabolism within membrane-bound organelles. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Dividing the cell into membrane-bound compartments allows reactions that would otherwise interfere to run at the same time in separate places — for example, digestion inside a lysosome can proceed while synthesis continues in the cytoplasm. Confining a pathway to a small compartment concentrates its enzymes and substrates together, so they collide and react more frequently and the reaction rate is higher. Harmful substances and destructive enzymes, such as the hydrolytic enzymes of a lysosome, are isolated behind a membrane so they do not damage the rest of the cell. Finally, each compartment can maintain its own optimal conditions — for instance the low pH inside a lysosome — set to suit the reactions it contains, and folded internal membranes provide extra surface area for membrane-bound reactions.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid advantage scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (A):** separates incompatible reactions, so they can occur simultaneously without interfering.
- **Point 2 (M):** concentrates enzymes and substrates in a small volume → higher collision frequency → higher reaction rate.
- **Point 3 (A):** isolates harmful substances / destructive enzymes, protecting the rest of the cell.
- **Point 4 (A):** allows localised optimal conditions (e.g. pH) suited to the reactions in that compartment.
- **Also creditable (A):** increases membrane surface area available for membrane-bound reactions.

Five creditable advantages are shown for a 4-mark question, so the engine caps the award at 4 — there is deliberate room to miss one and still score full marks. But the marks are for DISTINCT ideas: giving 'separates reactions' and 'stops reactions interfering' as two points scores only once, because they are the same advantage in different words.

**Equivalents and ECF.** The engine accepts equivalent wording — 'keeps enzymes and their substrates close together' earns the concentration mark just as '_concentrates_ enzymes and substrates' does. A correct advantage that a student explains slightly imperfectly can still take the mark if the valid idea is present, and because the points are independent, a weak or wrong first sentence does not stop the later advantages scoring (ECF).

**What the engine rejects.** A vague answer such as 'organelles make the cell more organised and efficient' scores nothing on its own, because it names no specific advantage. It also rejects examples that are not membrane-bound compartments — ribosomes, cell wall, cytoskeleton — since they do not illustrate compartmentalization.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, give four DISTINCT advantages from the list — separating incompatible reactions, concentrating enzymes/substrates for a higher rate, isolating harmful substances, localising optimal conditions, increasing membrane surface area — each stated clearly enough to be recognised on its own.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, one mark per distinct valid advantage, with equivalents accepted and ECF applied, capped at the marks available. So write in separate, creditable statements: name the advantage and, where the point calls for it, say WHY it helps (for the rate mark, spell out that concentrating enzymes and substrates increases collisions). Listing the same advantage twice in different words earns one mark, not two, so reach for a different item on the list each time.

Compartmentalization is the idea the rest of eukaryotic cell biology is built on. The membrane surfaces you meet here become the stage for the electron transport chain in respiration and the light-dependent reactions in photosynthesis; the endomembrane pathway reappears whenever a cell exports a protein, from antibodies to digestive enzymes; and the contrast with the single-compartment prokaryote frames the whole story of how eukaryotic cells grew larger and more complex. Master the habit of linking a structure to its function and giving distinct, named advantages, and you have a template that answers organelle and compartmentalization questions cleanly every time.
